story:
Julia, heat-drowsy and bored in her mum’s cavernous charity shop that smells of beeswax, mothballs, and old paper, corrals her twin sisters into hide-and-seek. Dust swims like overcooked pasta; the place brims with cabinets and forgotten treasure. A toppled box in the back spills dog‑eared magazines, faded photos…and a mangy Teddy Ruxpin. Something in the box shivers at the edge of sight. Then the bear’s eyelids and mouth jerk. A scream, footsteps, and Mum—hands on hips—arrives, scowl melting into delight. “A Teddy Ruxpin! I wanted one so badly.” She shows the girls the tape deck under its vest, finds a cartridge labeled “Ollie’s Story: The Bear (1986),” pops it into the slot to demonstrate—there’s a click, a cough of gears—and carts the whole box home, promising to sort it after her pub shift. Mildred will babysit.

Inside the bear, a mind wakes to stifling dark and sloshing motion. “Where am I?” becomes “Who am I?” A click, a fullness, memories rushing in. A child’s plea to play the tape. A label: “Ollie’s Story.” “It’s me,” the voice says. “I’m Ollie.”

At home, the twins beg to hear Teddy speak. Emma has already jabbed play; the bear’s mouth starts to chew words. Julia hits stop—too spooked—and starts tidying, but the bear keeps watching her. “Hey kid, help me up,” it says, voice tinny, oddly calm. Mildred clatters in the kitchen, head in the freezer hunting ice cream. Julia hustles the toy down the hall, shushing it. Back in the bedroom, the twins stand amid the box’s spill, clutching a discolored instant photo: a girl and a boy in front of a once-grand townhouse. The caption reads: “Victoria House – Edith & Oliver, 1986.” Julia frowns at it. “Edith Ainsworth died last week,” she says. “These are her hospice boxes from Victoria House.” “Here’s Teddy!” Emma announces, finger on the bear in the boy’s arms.

“No,” the bear snaps, rattled. “That’s Finn Fluff. I’m Ollie—Oliver. The boy.” The twins stare at his plush leg, at the seam where stuffing shows. “But you’re…a toy,” Ella whispers. Horror catches in Ollie’s throat. What comes out is a story—the one about the eighth-birthday summer when everything ended, when Didi (Edith) gave him Finn Fluff and the world tilted. He talks for hours, or a blink. As the tale dies, the tape ejects itself and powders to dust. Silence.

Late, Julia lies awake between the softly snoring twins. In the kitchen, murmurs. Mum and Mildred over tea. Edith Ainsworth died last week, Mum says; hospice boxes dumped at the shop; Victoria House already on the market cheap. Mildred remembers when the Ainsworths arrived, landed gentry turned threadbare. Then 1986: Edith promised Oliver a fair, but they never made it. Taken. Missing three weeks. Police, dogs, press. Ransom whispered. They returned, but the family cracked. Oliver was shipped to boarding school, later renounced the name; no one saw him again. Edith never left the house. Their father, Edmund, died in 2003. She lived alone two decades, fading into rumor. Some said Edmund’s gambling debts brought the trouble; no one proved it. “Why would Oliver forsake her when she needed him most?” Mildred asks.

Julia’s fingers tighten around a second tape she found at the box’s bottom: “Ollie’s Story: The Fair that Wasn’t (1986).” The first cartridge is gone, but the voice is not finished. Neither is she. She pads back to the bedroom, to the Teddy that insists he is a boy, to the story that wants to be told to its end.
